narration.default: So far our functions just DID things — printed messages — but didn't hand back any answer. Sometimes you want a function to compute something and HAND BACK the result so the caller can use it. Think of Scratch's "reporter blocks" — the round green blocks that GIVE BACK a value, like ((x) + (y)) or (sqrt of (9)). You can't just stand them on the script area; you have to slot them INTO another block. Same idea in C: a function that returns a value can be used wherever a value would normally fit — in math, in printf, anywhere.

Lines 3 to 5: int square(int n) { return n * n; }. Two big changes from last chapter's function. (1) The first int means "this function RETURNS an int" — it gives back a whole number when it finishes. Last chapter we used void (returns nothing); now we use int (returns an int). (2) Inside the body, return n * n; — return is the magic keyword. It calculates n times n and HANDS THAT VALUE back to whoever called square. The function exits the moment return runs.

Line 8: int result = square(5); — this is where the return value gets CAUGHT. C reads it as: "call square with 5, get back the answer, store it in result". square(5) computes 5 * 5 = 25, returns 25, the = stores 25 into result. Now the function call square(5) is acting like an EXPRESSION — like the number 25 itself. You can use a returning-function-call anywhere you'd use a number: in math, in conditions, in printf, in other function calls.

Click Run. Console: "5 squared is 25". Now try CHAINING: replace the printf with printf("Sum of squares: %d\n", square(7) + square(3));. square(7) returns 49, square(3) returns 9, the sum is 58. Reporter-block-style chaining works in C the same way it did in Scratch — function calls plug into bigger expressions.
